sections.heading: Violent discipline declined nationally, while non-violent discipline rose
sections.body: In 2025, 54.7 percent of children aged 1 to 14 years experienced any violent discipline method, down from 58.8 percent in 2022. The decline appears across the components: psychological aggression fell from 48.4 to 43.6 percent, any physical punishment from 39.1 to 36.1 percent, and severe physical punishment from 3.4 to 2.0 percent. At the same time, only non-violent discipline rose from 35.7 to 39.0 percent. The national picture is therefore one of improvement, but with violent discipline still affecting more than half of children.

sections.heading: Regional exposure ranged from about one-third to more than seven in ten children
sections.body: The regional spread in any violent discipline was wide in 2025, from 32.7 percent in BARMM and 34.9 percent in Central Visayas to 70.9 percent in Davao Region, 66.8 percent in Cagayan Valley, and 66.4 percent in both Bicol Region and Caraga. The same regions did not simply differ on one component. Davao Region combined the highest psychological aggression level at 60.3 percent with physical punishment at 44.7 percent, while BARMM had lower levels on both psychological aggression, at 27.7 percent, and physical punishment, at 19.9 percent. This spread is much larger than the national change over time.

sections.heading: Several regions improved sharply between 2022 and 2025
sections.body: The largest declines in any violent discipline were in Zamboanga Peninsula¹, Central Visayas, and Ilocos Region. Zamboanga Peninsula fell from 72.3 percent in 2022 to 41.9 percent in 2025, alongside large drops in psychological aggression from 60.0 to 34.7 percent and any physical punishment from 58.1 to 27.3 percent. Central Visayas fell from 59.9 to 34.9 percent, with only non-violent discipline rising from 39.3 to 59.4 percent. Ilocos Region also moved down from 66.3 to 52.3 percent, suggesting that regional change was not uniform but could be substantial where both psychological and physical components declined.

sections.heading: Other regions moved against the national decline
sections.body: Caraga increased from 59.7 percent in 2022 to 66.4 percent in 2025 for any violent discipline, with psychological aggression rising from 50.8 to 58.4 percent and any physical punishment from 32.0 to 38.9 percent. BARMM also rose, from 28.3 to 32.7 percent, even though it remained the lowest region in 2025; its increase was driven by psychological aggression rising from 23.3 to 27.7 percent while physical punishment fell from 21.6 to 19.9 percent. Davao Region remained very high, moving from 69.2 to 70.9 percent, with psychological aggression increasing and severe physical punishment declining. These mixed component movements show why the headline measure should be read with its underlying methods.

sections.heading: Severe physical punishment was low everywhere, but not absent
sections.body: Severe physical punishment was much less common than psychological aggression or any physical punishment in every region, but it still appeared across the map. The highest 2025 values were in Negros Island Region at 3.3 percent, Eastern Visayas and Northern Mindanao at 2.8 percent each, and Central Luzon and Caraga at 2.6 percent each. The lowest regional values were 1.0 percent in Ilocos Region and 1.2 percent in Davao Region. The inference is that the regional burden of violent discipline is mostly shaped by psychological aggression and non-severe physical punishment, while severe physical punishment remains a smaller but persistent component.

sections.heading: Children aged 3 to 9 had the highest exposure
sections.body: Age differences were clear in 2025. Any violent discipline was 42.3 percent among children aged 1 to 2 years, then rose to 59.2 percent among those aged 3 to 4 and 58.5 percent among those aged 5 to 9, before easing to 53.1 percent among children aged 10 to 14. The components show why: psychological aggression was much lower for ages 1 to 2 at 25.5 percent than for older groups, while physical punishment peaked at 46.2 percent among ages 3 to 4 and 42.2 percent among ages 5 to 9. All four age groups had lower violent discipline in 2025 than in 2022.

sections.heading: Boys had higher violent-discipline exposure than girls
sections.body: Male children had higher exposure to any violent discipline in 2025, at 57.1 percent, compared with 52.0 percent among female children. The gap appeared in both major components: psychological aggression was 45.4 percent for boys and 41.5 percent for girls, while any physical punishment was 38.9 percent for boys and 32.9 percent for girls. Severe physical punishment was also slightly higher for boys, at 2.3 percent compared with 1.8 percent for girls. Both sexes improved from 2022, but the male-female gap remained.

sections.heading: The urban-rural gap was modest, with urban children slightly higher
sections.body: Urban children had a 2025 any violent discipline level of 56.2 percent, compared with 53.1 percent among rural children. Psychological aggression was nearly the same in urban and rural areas, at 43.8 and 43.4 percent, but physical punishment was higher in urban areas, at 39.1 percent versus 32.9 percent. Only non-violent discipline was higher in rural areas, at 40.8 percent compared with 37.4 percent in urban areas. Both residence groups had lower violent discipline than in 2022, so the difference is smaller than the regional divide.

sections.heading: Mother's education did not form a simple downward gradient
sections.body: In 2025, any violent discipline ranged from 46.1 percent among children whose mothers had no education to 58.8 percent among those whose mothers had post-secondary education and 58.1 percent among those whose mothers had Grades 7 to 10. Children whose mothers had college education were at 53.5 percent, while children whose mothers were not in the household were at 49.4 percent. Component patterns also varied: post-secondary had the highest psychological aggression level at 50.9 percent, while Grades 11 to 12 and post-secondary had the highest physical punishment levels at 43.9 percent. The data do not support a simple interpretation that more maternal schooling always corresponds to lower violent discipline.

sections.heading: Wealth differences peaked in the fourth quintile, not at the bottom
sections.body: The highest 2025 level of any violent discipline was in the fourth wealth quintile at 60.4 percent, while the highest quintile had the lowest level at 47.2 percent. The middle quintile was also elevated at 56.4 percent, followed by the second at 55.4 percent and the lowest at 53.4 percent. Psychological aggression and physical punishment followed a similar pattern, with the fourth quintile highest on both measures. Compared with 2022, the largest declines were in the highest, middle, and second quintiles, while the fourth quintile was nearly unchanged.

takeaways: Any violent discipline declined nationally from 58.8 percent in 2022 to 54.7 percent in 2025, but it still affected more than half of children aged 1 to 14 years.
Regional differences were large, ranging in 2025 from 32.7 percent in BARMM to 70.9 percent in Davao Region.
Psychological aggression and any physical punishment drove most of the headline regional differences, while severe physical punishment was much less common.
Children aged 3 to 9, boys, urban children, and children in the fourth wealth quintile had higher 2025 exposure than their comparison groups.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
